author: @DigGil3
text:
I've suspected that this would work, but never tested it.

For those wanting to find more about how this works, here's something to start with: https://youtu.be/xyMH8wKK-Ag
The capacitor will change the impedance of the coil it is connected to and thus make its current intensity waveform lag behind of the other coils.

author: @suffolkcountysheriff
text: Just donated 100 US to the go fund me site. How can I purchase your books with US currency? Also thoughts on intercollated phosphorous for phosphorene production?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: suffolkcountysheriff Hiya mate, Thanks very much. I am aware of phosphorene - i read the paper when it came out - but I haven't really looked at and to be honest it strikes me as a long way from being useful so I haven't looked any further and I am not likely to in the near future - It is interesting but so are all the 'sheet like' materials - molybdenum disulphide for example - which i think is much more useful - unfortunately it is just not possible for me to look at everything and i therefore only look at what i think is interesting - this could mean i am missing stuff - but so be it - what i do look at is interesting enough for me lol

replies.author: @suffolkcountysheriff
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith I hear you, so many cool things so little time.  So two more things.  1) how do I purchase your books with US currency  (dollars).  2) I am in the process of building a lab/work shop. I was wondering if you could do a video of basic equipment to stock it with. Heated stirrers, kilns.. etc. Bottom line I would like to start doing my own experiments based on your work and make videos as well. So far I have a 12ftx12ftx12ft area, insulated, heated and cooled, 7000 watts electric service, water. I haven't finished the interior yet and before I do I want to design the layout around what equipment I will have in it. Basically I'm looking for some guidance.

Send me an e-mail address mate and i will just send you the books - as a thank you for your donation - there is one that is very very useful but it is around 50 dollars - there is a ton of good info in it and it's a bit technical - hence the price - but dude - i really owe you one lol

author: @erlendse
text:
Neat motors but tricky. 
Technically they are 3 phase synchronous motor, but you are not wrong.

I have found a not too tricky to connect up controller chip (tda5145) that I like to use(good for 10-15W). Bldc controllers in general is what it takes.
There is also the vector/field oriented control stuff, that use a mix of magic and math as far as I can tell.

Otherwise there are the electronic rc speed controllers, I still think that name is wrong.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: erlendse can you do a schematic for me mate - i'd like to follow that one up

replies.author: @ErlendErvik
replies.text: Sure.. but I have only used the excample in the datasheet mostly. You may have to check ebay and aliexpress etc to get the part.. rather obsolete

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Erlend Ervik don't worry about it mate - i'll just dig up the datasheet - though i was thinking of using a hall effect latch

replies.author: @ErlendErvik
replies.text:
It's no problem :)

I rather see HDD motors be used for stuff.

The HAL-route is technically best, but require 3 sensors (some let you get away with one).

That chip I mentioned is a block-driver, it senses one wire compared to center and drive the 2 other. Swapping the roles as the motor spin.
If you don't have the center-wire, a WYE resistor network may do the job.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Erlend Ervik i was aiming for super simple non frightening almost plug and play with this - technical comes later lol
